data.title: Pantheon Resources PLC Announces Granting Restricted Stock Units and Blocklisting Application
data.description:
LONDON, UK / ACCESS Newswire / April 13, 2026 / Pantheon Resources plc ("Pantheon" or the "Company"), the oil and gas company developing the Kodiak and Ahpun projects on Alaska's North Slope, announces the annual granting of restricted stock units ("RSUs") pursuant to Pantheon's Employee Stock Ownership Plan ("ESOP"), as announced in 2024 and 2025.

Grant of RSUs

In October 2024, Pantheon announced details of its replacement ESOP ("2024 ESOP") for all employees and a Long-Term Incentive Plan ("LTIP") for Executive Directors and certain officers of the Company. The Company retired its remaining share option-based incentive plan (originally adopted in 2009 and amended in 2014) with the ESOP which, where practicable, looks to follow the principles of the Main Market of the London Stock Exchange.For further details on the ESOP, please refer to Note 23 within the Company's Consolidated Financial Statements for the financial year ended 30 June 2025, as included in the Company's Annual Report for that year.

Under the terms of the ESOP and as approved by the Company's Remuneration Committee, the Company has issued in aggregate 12,960,102 RSUs across all staff members (excluding non-executive directors). The number of RSUs awarded has been calculated using a price of £0.1160 being the closing share price at 8 April 2026 and the RSUs will vest equally in three annual tranches commencing 9 April 2027 and subject to certain conditions. Details of the RSUs granted to Persons Discharged with Managerial Responsibilities (PDMRs) are disclosed in the table below.

PDMR

2026 RSUs Granted

2026 RSUs as a % of Issued Share Capital

Max Easley

1,764,037

0.12%

Tralisa Maraj

1,363,119

0.09%

Erich Krumanocher

1,347,082

0.09%

Blocklisting Application

An application has been made to the London Stock Exchange for an additional headroom of 8,000,000 Ordinary Shares of £0.01 each (the "New Ordinary Shares") to be added to the existing block listing, as announced on 24 July 2025, (the "2024 ESOP Blocklisting"). It is expected that admission will become effective on or around 16 April 2026.

The New Ordinary Shares will be issued where necessary, in order to satisfy the issue of shares pursuant to the future exercise of previously granted LTIP options and the future vesting of previously granted RSU's (and possible RSU grants to future hires) under the ESOP. When issued, the New Ordinary Shares will be credited as fully paid and will rank pari passu in all respects with the existing Ordinary Shares of £0.01 each in the capital of the Company ("Ordinary Shares"). The Company will provide bi-annual updates in respect of the 2024 ESOP Blocklisting.

The Company notes that the 2024 ESOP Blocklisting will continue to run in tandem to the Company's existing blocklisting, which will remain in place to satisfy the exercise of options under the Pantheon Resources Plc 2009 Discretionary Share Option Plan.

data.title: Bedtime M&A uncovers big dealmaker dreams
data.description:
By Jonathan Guilford

After beating back the FTC to combine mattress making and retailing, Somnigroup wants to expand further. With a soaring valuation, it is buying Legett & Platt for $2.5 bln to own springs and foam too. It's the sort of market-power move that only gets by sleepy trustbusters.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Jonathan Guilford on X and LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

Somnigroup, owner of mattress brand Tempur Sealy and retailer Mattress Firm, said on April 13 it had agreed to buy foam and inner-spring supplier Leggett & Platt in a $2.5 billion all-stock deal.

Under the terms of the transaction, Leggett & Platt stockholders will receive 0.1455 shares of Somnigroup for each share held, worth about $11.36 apiece based on undisturbed trading prices.

Somnigroup first publicly proposed to acquire Leggett & Platt in December.

Goldman Sachs is advising Somnigroup and JPMorgan is advising Leggett & Platt.

data.title: Growth or Inflation? Central banks could face a no-win choice
data.description:
Key points:
  • Nasdaq up ~0.6%, S&P 500 up ~0.4%; Dow flat
  • Tech and Financials lead S&P 500 sector gainers; Utiltiies weakest group
  • Dollar, gold slip; bitcoin rises >1%; US crude rallies ~4%
  • US 10-Year Treasury yield edges down to ~4.31%

GROWTH OR INFLATION? CENTRAL BANKS COULD FACE A NO-WIN CHOICE

Central banks worldwide are confronting a difficult balancing act: stimulating economic growth while keeping inflation under control. When inflation coincides with slowing growth, policymakers face an especially thorny dilemma—deciding which threat deserves priority.

Philip Palumbo, founder, CEO and chief investment officer of Palumbo Wealth Management, argues that central banks, particularly the U.S. Federal Reserve, are more concerned about weak growth than stubbornly high inflation. “Reigniting growth is a tougher road than reducing inflation,” he wrote in a recent note.

As a result, Palumbo believes policymakers are likely to act first to support growth, even if it means tolerating higher inflation in the near term. He describes this approach as “the path of least resistance.”

Palumbo adds that prolonged geopolitical tensions, including the ongoing conflict involving Iran, could further weigh on economic momentum. Under such circumstances, he expects interest rates to eventually move lower, a shift that could leave inflation elevated or even push it higher. While inflation typically poses challenges for equity valuations, Palumbo notes that markets often adjust over time. Because corporate revenues and earnings can rise alongside prices, stocks may ultimately remain among the more attractive investment options during inflationary periods.

He also suggests commodities generally perform well when inflation accelerates, while bonds tend to suffer due to the declining purchasing power of money. With the direction of geopolitical events seemingly changing by the hour, Palumbo’s advice is straightforward: ignore headline noise and maintain confidence in the underlying resilience of the U.S. economy.

data.title: Streaming viewers are OK with double the commercials as 'subscription fatigue' sets in
data.description:
By Lukas I. Alpert

As streaming costs go up, viewers are looking for better deals - and many say they'd watch more ads for lower prices, a new survey shows

People's willingness to watch more ads on streaming TV is going up as prices have been rising, a new survey shows.

"Peak TV" is now being replaced by "subscription fatigue."

The number of viewers who say they are willing to watch more ads in order to get better deals is rising, according to a new survey, as prices continue to go up for many streaming services.

With overall costs rising for consumers, many are looking for savings wherever they can - and the survey shows that more than a third of streaming customers say they would watch twice as many ads in exchange for lower prices.

"For years, the assumption was that subscribers would always pay more to avoid adverts. But for a growing number of consumers, watching more ads is now an acceptable trade-off if it means keeping monthly costs down," said Giles Tongue, a subscription expert with Bango, a subscription-bundling service in the U.K. which commissioned the survey.

In a poll of 2,500 streaming customers in the U.S. that was conducted in January, Bango found that 36% said they would be happy to watch more advertising in return for lower prices. That's up from 24% who didn't object to advertising on streaming services in 2024.

The preference is even more pronounced now among younger viewers, with 46% of millennials and 49% of Gen Z respondents saying they were willing to watch more ads.

The shift to ad-driven packages comes as many streaming services have been raising prices. Just last month, Netflix (NFLX) increased its prices for the second time in just over a year, by $1 per month for its ad-supported tier and $2 a month for its standard and premium plans.

Amazon Prime (AMZN) also recently announced it was raising prices for viewers to watch ad-free to $4.99, from $2.99 a month (on top of the annual Prime subscription fee). Paramount+ (PSKY) also raised prices by $1 a month across its range of subscription plans.

Bango's survey revealed that the average American consumer is paying $69 a month - or $828 a year - for steaming services. Almost a quarter of them said they were spending more than they could afford on such services, with 41% of Gen Z-ers saying they were exceeding their limits on streaming.

The pressure on consumers comes as the growth of streaming services has slowed, with much of it coming from viewers shifting over from cable rather than an actual expansion of the market.

Data show that overall consumer spending on video entertainment - which includes streaming services and cable TV - grew very slightly between 2019 and 2025, from $140 billion to $144 billion.

That adds up to a compounded annual growth rate of just 0.4% since 2019 - but when inflation is factored in, that figure drops to minus 3%.

This has led streaming services to look for ways to increase profitability, mainly by cutting costs and raising prices.

-Lukas I. Alpert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: EU Antitrust Officials Inspect Chocolate Company's Premises
data.description:
By Edith Hancock

The European Commission said it is carrying out unannounced inspections at the premises of a chocolate company in two member states, citing concerns over a potential breach of the bloc's competition rules.

The commission--the EU's executive arm--said on Monday it is concerned the company might have broken EU antitrust rules related to cartels and restrictive business practices as well as rules related to a company abusing its dominant market position. It didn't name the company.

The searches are part of the regulator's scrutiny into a case of potential market segmentation, which includes restricting trade of goods between EU member states and barriers to multicountry purchases.

The commission can carry out inspections in the early stages of scrutinizing a company's business practices.

Write to Edith Hancock at edith.hancock@wsj.com

data.title: Mail-order dominates US abortion pill dispensing
data.description:
By Ahmed Aboulenein

U.S. pharmacies are largely filling prescriptions for the abortion pill mifepristone via mail-order in states permitting telehealth access to the medication, despite a 2023 FDA decision allowing retail pharmacies to dispense the drug, according to a study published on Monday.

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration in 2023 authorized retail pharmacies to provide mifepristone, the first of two pills used in medication abortion, during the first 10 weeks of pregnancy. This followed a prior rule change permitting the drug to be shipped by mail without in-person dispensing requirements.

Research from the University of Southern California published in the medical journal JAMA showed that since January 2023, pharmacies have filled about 2,700 mifepristone prescriptions monthly. In the 27 states and the District of Columbia where telehealth abortion is allowed, fewer than 2% of those prescriptions were filled at physical retail pharmacies.

In 11 states where abortion is legal but telehealth access is restricted, some 61% of prescriptions were filled at in-store pharmacies, the study found.

The findings underscore the significance of mail-order dispensing for mifepristone after the U.S. Supreme Court overturned the constitutional right to abortion in 2022, allowing states to ban or restrict the procedure. Researchers also noted substantial access disparities even in states with relatively robust abortion protections.

"It's striking how few retail pharmacies, particularly chains, are dispensing mifepristone where abortion is legal and telehealth is permitted," said Dima Qato, the study's lead author and senior scholar at the University of Southern California's Schaeffer Institute.

Pharmacy chains have come under pressure from both abortion rights advocates and anti-abortion groups following the FDA policy revision.

Restrictions implemented after the Supreme Court's ruling have driven a surge in medication abortion, which now accounts for 60% of all U.S. abortions. Nearly half of U.S. states have banned or severely limited abortion, fueling legal disputes at state and federal levels, and a push by anti-abortion activists to ban the pills as well.

Last week, a federal judge temporarily halted Louisiana's lawsuit seeking to restrict nationwide access to mifepristone. Senate Republicans are investigating mifepristone manufacturers and have urged the FDA to curb online sales of the drug, which the agency approved over 25 years ago.

data.title: Insurers Stand to Gain Big Business From Data Centers — WSJ
data.description:
By Heather Gillers

$10 Billion

That's how much S&P Global Ratings estimates data centers could pay in insurance premiums this year. That number compares to $5 billion a year for the entire aviation sector, the ratings firm said in a report Monday.

The firm estimates the total insurable amount of data-center assets at $2 trillion worldwide and said it could increase by $300 billion in 2027. That is "a huge growth opportunity" for insurers and reinsurers, the ratings firm wrote.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Cloudflare Shares Rise Amid Expansion of Autonomous AI Tools
data.description:
By Christopher Kuo

Cloudflare shares climbed after the cloud connectivity company unveiled improvements to tools that will enable developers to build and deploy artificial intelligence agents.

The stock was up 9% $181.83 in midday trading on Monday. Shares have risen 70% in the past year.

Cloudflare said Agent Cloud will provide developers with infrastructure, computing, deployment and security tools for designing AI agents, which are models that can code, reason and complete tasks autonomously. While most AI development has focused on chatbots in recent years, autonomous agents are the future of AI innovation, Cloudflare said.

One of Cloudflare's tools will give AI agents their own computers through an isolated Linux environment, the company said. Cloudflare executives said they want their tools to enable people to run dozens of personal agents simultaneously.

Write to Christopher Kuo at chris.kuo@wsj.com

data.title: GSK Plans Five Late-Stage Cancer Trials As Shares Rise Nearly 50%
data.description:
GSK is beginning to show a more assertive shift in strategy as CEO Luke Miels pushes the company toward higher-risk oncology development, with plans to launch five simultaneous late-stage trials for its cancer drug mocertatug rezetecan, known as Mo-Rez. The program includes three studies in ovarian cancer and two in endometrial cancer, a scale that could cost tens of millions of dollars per trial and marks a notable departure from the company's historically cautious approach. Since Miels' appointment was announced in September, GSK's shares have risen close to 50%, outperforming European pharmaceutical peers, although the stock slipped as much as 1.2% in early London trading on Monday amid a broader market decline.

The move could position GSK closer to competitors such as AstraZeneca , which has accelerated oncology development through multiple late-stage programs over recent years. GSK had largely exited cancer drug development in 2015 before re-entering through acquisitions including Tesaro in 2019 and Sierra Oncology in 2022, with a focus on gynecologic and blood cancers. According to Chief Scientific Officer Tony Wood, the company is becoming more agile in advancing its pipeline, and Mo-Rez is among the first examples of that shift. Early-stage data presented at the Society of Gynecologic Oncology's annual meeting showed overall response rates of 62% in ovarian cancer patients resistant to certain chemotherapy and 67% in endometrial cancer patients, suggesting the drug could have meaningful potential if confirmed in later-stage trials.

For investors, the strategy introduces both potential upside and execution risk, particularly given the scale and timing of late-stage development. Outcomes at this stage have historically had significant impact on peers such as Sanofi, where unfavorable results have weighed on performance. Still, early signals have been strong enough for Jefferies to increase its revenue estimates for Mo-Rez by $2 billion, pointing to what could be a sizable market opportunity. The drug, an antibody-drug conjugate licensed from Hansoh Pharma, is designed to deliver targeted cancer-killing compounds directly into tumors, and management has indicated growing confidence in its efficacy and safety profile as development accelerates under Miels' leadership.
